input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** being studied is a **complex engineered system** (e.g., manufacturing or industrial system) characterized by the need for **multi-level abstraction modeling** (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers). The system is designed to integrate **heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives**, requiring **interoperability** across diverse modeling notations and tools. Key characteristics include:
  - **Modularity**: Decomposed into **components and connectors** (e.g., via Architecture Description Languages like ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML Internal Block Diagrams).
  - **Behavioral Complexity**: Described using **state machines** (SysML v1.7), **automata** (MontiArc Automaton), or **simulation tools** (e.g., MathWorks Simulink, other simulation engines).
  - **Cross-Domain Integration**: Combines **functional/logical architectures** (ADLs) with **simulation** (e.g., physics-based or logic-driven) and **general-purpose programming** (C++, Java).
  - **Purpose-Driven Data Collection**: Aligns with **Digital Shadow** principles (one-way data flow from physical to digital), but extends to **active processing** (e.g., via Digital Twin components for interpretation, optimization, or control).
  - **Operational Domain**: Manufacturing or industrial systems where **real-time data aggregation** and **contextual abstraction** are critical for decision-making or process optimization.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** provides the following **specific services** to users and the physical system, categorized by functionality and target stakeholders:
  - **Monitoring**: Passive **one-way data ingestion** from the physical system (as per Digital Shadow principles), with **automated aggregation and abstraction** of contextual data traces (e.g., sensor readings, operational logs).
  - **Data Interpretation & Processing**: **Active components** (contrasted with Digital Shadows) to **interpret incoming data**, enabling **real-time or near-real-time analysis** (e.g., via simulation tools like Simulink or custom logic in C++/Java).
  - **Prediction & Optimization**: Leverages **simulation models** (e.g., physics-based or logic-driven) to generate **predictive insights** (e.g., failure forecasting, performance optimization) or **recommendations** for operational adjustments.
  - **Control & Automation**: Enables **closed-loop interactions** (unlike Digital Shadows) by **pushing processed data back** to the physical system (e.g., via control algorithms or automated workflows).
  - **Visualization & Decision Support**: Provides **multi-perspective views** (e.g., functional, logical, or technical layers) to stakeholders using **integrated modeling tools** (e.g., SysML, ADLs) or **simulation dashboards** (e.g., Simulink).
  - **Stakeholder-Specific Services**:
    - **Engineers**: Access to **functional/logical architecture models** (ADLs) for system design or troubleshooting.
    - **Operators**: **Real-time operational dashboards** with aggregated data traces and predictive alerts.
    - **Managers**: **High-level abstractions** (e.g., KPIs, performance metrics) derived from lower-level data.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin implementation** relies on the following **specific tools, technologies, and enablers**, categorized by their roles in modeling, simulation, and integration:
  - **Modeling & Architecture Description**:
    - **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)**: Used for **multi-level abstraction** (requirements, functional/logical architectures, product layers) and **behavioral modeling** (state machines in v1.7).
    - **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)**: **ACME**, **AADL**, **MontiArc** (with **MontiArc Automaton** for behavioral modeling), or **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** to describe **components, connectors, interfaces, and composition**.
    - **Methodologies**: **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, or **SMArDT** for structuring complex system development.
  - **Simulation & Behavioral Modeling**:
    - **MathWorks Simulink**: For **physics-based or logic-driven simulation**, enabling **predictive analysis** or **control system design**.
    - **General-Purpose Simulation Tools**: Custom or third-party engines (e.g., [29], [30]) for **detailed behavioral modeling**.
  - **Programming & Integration**:
    - **General-Purpose Languages**: **C++** or **Java** for implementing **custom logic**, **control algorithms**, or **data processing pipelines**.
    - **Interoperability Frameworks**: Mechanisms to **integrate heterogeneous models/tools** (e.g., SysML ↔ Simulink ↔ ADLs ↔ code), addressing **stakeholder heterogeneity** [31].
  - **Data & Digital Shadow Enablers**:
    - **Digital Shadow Reference Model**: Defined via **UML class diagrams** (Fig. 1), formalizing **one-way data flow** from physical to digital objects, with **aggregation/abstraction** for specific purposes (e.g., manufacturing).
    - **Active Processing Components**: Contrasted with Digital Shadows, DTs include **software modules** to **interpret, optimize, or control** based on ingested data (e.g., via simulation or code).
  - **Development & Collaboration**:
    - **MBSE (Model-Based Systems Engineering)**: Overarching framework to **manage complexity** across **multiple modeling notations/tools** and **stakeholder perspectives**.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The provided document does not explicitly define multiplicities or hierarchical structures for Digital Twin (DT) instances. However, the context implies a **decentralized or modular architecture** is necessary to integrate heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives, as mentioned in the MBSE framework (Section II.A). The document does not specify centralized vs. decentralized architectures, coordination mechanisms, or scope of responsibilities for individual DT instances, but the use of **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like ACME, AADL, or MontiArc suggests support for **component-based decomposition** and **compositional hierarchies**. The document also references **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** for logical architecture, which may imply **multi-instance modeling** but lacks explicit details on synchronization or multiplicity constraints.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The **Digital Twin constellation** is implicitly described through the **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** framework (Section II.A), which emphasizes **orchestration via multi-notation modeling** (e.g., SysML for architecture, Simulink/MontiArc for behavior, and general-purpose languages like C++/Java for implementation). The document highlights the need for **system-wide coordination** to integrate **heterogeneous tools and stakeholders**, suggesting an **event-driven or service-oriented architecture** for dynamic interactions. While no explicit orchestration pattern (e.g., microservices, publish-subscribe) is defined, the **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (Section II.B) implies that DTs must include **active components** (e.g., interpreters, processors) to handle bidirectional data flows, contrasting with the passive nature of digital shadows. The document does not specify resource management or exact coordination mechanisms but implies **modular integration** via ADLs and simulation tools.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The document does not provide explicit details on **horizontal integration** (e.g., data exchange protocols, interoperability standards). However, the **MBSE framework** (Section II.A) requires integration across **multiple modeling notations and tools** (e.g., SysML, Simulink, C++/Java), suggesting dependencies on **standardized interfaces** (e.g., FMI for co-simulation, OPC UA for industrial data exchange) are implied but not named. The **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (Section II.B) highlights **one-way data flows** between physical and digital objects, but the DT’s role as an **active processor** (Section II.B) implies it must support **bidirectional integration** with external systems. No specific protocols (e.g., REST, MQTT) or enterprise system integration patterns (e.g., ERP, MES) are mentioned, but the **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** requirement (Section II.A) implies a need for **interoperability standards** to bridge disparate tools.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) system leverages **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies to integrate heterogeneous computational models and data components across multiple abstraction layers, including **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. Key modeling languages and tools include:
  - **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)** for structural and behavioral modeling, including **Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** for component-connector perspectives and **state machines** (SysML v1.7) for dynamic behavior.
  - **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME, AADL, and MontiArc**, which define system components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition, with extensions like **MontiArc Automaton** for behavioral modeling.
  - **Simulation tools** (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink**) and **general-purpose programming languages** (e.g., C++, Java) for detailed behavioral and analytical modeling.
  Data sources are aggregated from **physical systems** (via sensors/actuators) and processed through **model relationships** (e.g., hierarchical decomposition, interface mappings) to ensure consistency across tools. The **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (Kritzinger et al.) frames data as **contextual traces** (e.g., sensor readings, operational logs) abstracted for specific purposes, while the DT integrates these with **active components** (e.g., simulation engines, control logic) to enable bidirectional data flow and autonomous decision-making.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The Digital Twin system incorporates **fidelity requirements** aligned with MBSE methodologies, where model accuracy is validated through **multi-layered abstraction** (requirements → functional → logical → physical). **Validation and verification (V&V)** methods include:
  - **Model integration checks** across heterogeneous tools (e.g., SysML, Simulink) to ensure consistency in component interfaces and behavioral logic.
  - **Simulation-based validation** using tools like **Simulink** or domain-specific simulators to quantify deviations between digital and physical system states.
  - **Uncertainty quantification** via statistical analysis of sensor data traces (as defined in the Digital Shadow Reference Model) and model parameters, with error correction mechanisms tied to **active DT components** (e.g., adaptive calibration algorithms).
  Quality assurance processes rely on **stakeholder-specific perspectives** (e.g., engineers, operators) and **traceability matrices** linking requirements to implemented models. The **Digital Shadow’s passive data aggregation** serves as a baseline for identifying discrepancies, while the DT’s active components enforce **real-time fidelity adjustments** through feedback loops.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in a Digital Twin (DT) system is characterized by an **automated one-way data flow** from the physical object to its digital representation, as defined in the Digital Shadow Reference Model [32]. This flow involves the **collection of contextual data traces** (e.g., sensor readings, operational states, environmental parameters) from the physical system, followed by **aggregation, abstraction, and preprocessing** to ensure relevance and consistency. The data types transmitted may include structured (e.g., time-series sensor data, discrete event logs) and unstructured (e.g., multimedia logs) formats, depending on the application domain (e.g., manufacturing). **Communication protocols** are not explicitly specified in the document, but typical industrial implementations may leverage **OPC UA, MQTT, or AMQP** for real-time data transmission. **Transmission frequencies** depend on the use case (e.g., high-frequency for process monitoring, event-triggered for anomalies). **Data quality assurance** mechanisms (e.g., validation rules, anomaly detection) are implied but not detailed, while **preprocessing steps** (e.g., filtering, normalization, feature extraction) are necessary to align raw data with the DT’s abstraction layer. **Data integration mechanisms** involve combining heterogeneous data sources (e.g., PLCs, IoT devices) into a unified digital model, often using **ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) pipelines** or middleware like **Apache Kafka** for event-driven architectures. The document emphasizes that the digital shadow (a subset of DT functionality) operates passively, requiring **external software systems** to interpret and process the incoming data [33], [34].
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The **technical network infrastructure** for a Digital Twin (DT) system is not explicitly detailed in the provided document. However, the context implies the need for **heterogeneous communication protocols** to support interactions between physical assets, digital models, and external systems. While specific protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, HTTP/REST) are not mentioned, the document highlights the complexity of integrating **multiple modeling tools and stakeholders** [31], suggesting a **hybrid network topology** (e.g., star, mesh, or hierarchical) to accommodate diverse components. **Bandwidth and latency requirements** are use-case-dependent but likely prioritize **low-latency, high-reliability** for real-time control applications. **Reliability and redundancy mechanisms** (e.g., failover protocols, data replication) are implied but not specified, as are **security protocols** (e.g., encryption, authentication). The document does not provide details on network architecture (e.g., edge-cloud integration) or specific technologies like 5G or industrial Ethernet.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The **deployment infrastructure** for a Digital Twin (DT) system is not explicitly described in the provided document. However, the context of **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** and **heterogeneous tool integration** [31] suggests that hosting platforms may include **cloud (e.g., AWS IoT, Azure Digital Twins), edge (e.g., local gateways for real-time processing), or on-premise** solutions, depending on latency, security, and compliance requirements. **Computational and storage requirements** are implied to be substantial, given the need to process and store **multi-modal data traces** (e.g., sensor data, simulation logs) and **execute complex models** (e.g., physics-based simulations in Simulink [28]). **Scalability mechanisms** (e.g., containerization with Docker/Kubernetes, auto-scaling) are likely required to handle dynamic workloads, though specifics are absent. The document does not mention **deployment models** (e.g., microservices, monolithic) or **availability/reliability assurances** (e.g., SLAs, redundancy strategies).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Digital Twin (DT) development process leverages **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, utilizing **systems engineering modeling languages** such as **SysML** for structuring system descriptions across multiple abstraction layers (e.g., requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers). Methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, or **SMArDT** are employed to manage complexity. **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME**, **AADL**, **MontiArc**, or **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** are used to model components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavioral descriptions are enhanced with tools like **MathWorks Simulink**, other simulation tools (e.g., [29], [30]), or general-purpose programming languages (e.g., **C++**, **Java**). The process integrates heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives and toolchains, requiring coordination across modeling notations and tools. **Quality assurance** is implicitly tied to the rigor of MBSE methodologies, while **version control** and **evolution management** are not explicitly detailed in the provided context. The transition between phases (e.g., from logical to physical architecture) relies on iterative refinement and validation via simulation or code generation, though governance approaches are not specified.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The provided documents reference **cybersecurity protocols** and **access control mechanisms** implicitly through the **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** framework (referenced in [46], [47], [50]), which is a key component of **RAMI4.0** ([49]). The AAS defines a standardized structure for digital twins in Industrie 4.0, incorporating **authentication systems** via **OAuth 2.0** and **JWT (JSON Web Tokens)** for secure communication between assets and their digital twins ([50]). Additionally, the **Functional Mockup Interface (FMI)** ([51])—used for exchanging simulation models—implies **fail-safe mechanisms** through standardized model validation and error handling protocols. **Threat mitigation strategies** are indirectly addressed via the **Asset Administration Shell’s** modular design, which supports **role-based access control (RBAC)** and **encryption** (e.g., TLS/SSL) for data integrity ([50]). However, no explicit **risk assessment approaches** or **safety-critical system considerations** (e.g., IEC 61508 compliance) are detailed in the provided documents. The **MQTT protocol** ([52]), referenced for IoT communication, includes **authentication and TLS support**, but no granular threat modeling or compliance frameworks (e.g., ISO 27001) are specified.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

[41] M. Konersmann, J. Michael, and B. Rumpe, “Towards Reference Models
with Conformance Relations for Structure,” in _Informing Possible Future_
_Worlds. Essays in Honour of Ulrich Frank_ . Logos Verlag Berlin, 2024,
pp. 247–269.

[42] M. Heithoff, C. Hopmann, T. Köbel, J. Michael, B. Rumpe, and P. Sapel,
“Application of Digital Shadows on Different Levels in the Automation
Pyramid,” _Data and Knowledge Engineering (DKE)_, vol. 158, 2025.

[43] M. Heithoff, J. Michael, B. Rumpe, J. Pfeiffer, A. Wortmann, and
J. Zhang, “A Method for Model-Driven Engineering of Digital Twins
in Manufacturing,” in _28th ACM/IEEE Int. Conf. on Model Driven_
_Engineering Languages and Systems_ . IEEE, 2025.

[44] MABI, “Mabi robot,” [https://www.mabi-robotic.com/en/robotic/](https://www.mabi-robotic.com/en/robotic/robotic-arm/max-100-2-25-p)
[robotic-arm/max-100-2-25-p, 2025, accessed: 2025-07-04.](https://www.mabi-robotic.com/en/robotic/robotic-arm/max-100-2-25-p)

[45] M. Trinh, R. Schwiedernoch, L. Gründel, S. Storms, and C. Brecher,
“Friction modeling for structured learning of robot dynamics,” in _Lecture_
_Notes in Production Engineering_ . Springer, 2023, pp. 396–406.

[46] S. R. Bader and M. Maleshkova, “The semantic asset administration
shell,” in _Semantic Systems. The Power of AI and Knowledge Graphs:_
